Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Dairy

**Milk** is a nutritious liquid produced by mammals, not containing colostrum.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3) In English and Western countries, the term 'milk' used alone almost always refers to cow's milk.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

## Source

[edit | edit source]

While all mammals produce milk, humans only regularly harvest and consume the milk of a few species.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) These include cows, goats, sheep, camels, yaks, and water buffalo.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Worldwide, cow milk is the most consumed,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) and it is the most prevalent variety in many western countries, including North America.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) South and tropical Asia source a large proportion of milk from water buffalo,[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) and a good amount of milk in Africa and the Middle East comes from sheep, goats, and camels.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) Yak milk is available in the region around Tibet.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) In areas where cow milk is the dominant variety, other varieties may still be used for specialized purposes.[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

### Composition

[edit | edit source]

The main constituent of milk is water,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) which comes to over 80% by weight. After this, milk contains a number of nutrients including carbohydrates, proteins, fats, and vitamins/minerals.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) The bulk of milk's carbohydrates take the form of lactose (milk sugar),[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) which has only a mildly sweet flavor.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) As far as fat goes, milk fat is largely saturated,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) especially in milk from ruminants.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Generally, the type and overall content of fat depends on a variety of factors, including species, diet, and season.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) In many regions, milk (usually cow milk) produced by large daries is classified according to its fat content.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) For example, in the United States, **whole** milk contains 3–4% fat;[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16) **reduced-fat** contains 2%;[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16) **low-fat** contains 1%;[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16) and **fat-free/skim** milk contains below 0.1–0.5%.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16) Most of the milk proteins are classified as either curd/casein proteins or whey proteins.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) The whey proteins are water-soluble and can coagulate with heat but not acid.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) The caseins, on the other hand, are susceptible to coagulation with the application of acid, and this is what makes cheese curds.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) Milk also contains many functional enzymes when fresh, though these are often denatured by heating.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) Milk is very slightly acidic.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)

Ultimately, the precise composition of a given milk will depend on a variety of factors, including species, breed, individual, age, season, diet, health, lactation stage, time of day, and even geography.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[17]](#cite_note-:13-17)[[18]](#cite_note-:24-18) For example, sheep milk is particularly high in protein and fat compared to cow or goat milk.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[18]](#cite_note-:24-18) The table below gives the approximate component breakdown of unmodified milk from different animal species.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[18]](#cite_note-:24-18)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19)

| Origin | Water | Fat | Protein | Carbohydrate | Sugars | Minerals |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cow | 85–91% | 3–5.2% | 3–4% | 4.6–5.2% | 4.6–5% | 0.7–1.7% |
| Sheep | 80.7% | 7.2–7.9% | 4.6% |  | 4.8% | 0.9% |
| Goat | 87% | 4.1–6% | 3.2% |  | 4.3% | 0.8% |
| Buffalo | 82.2% | 7.5% |  |  |  |  |
| Yak |  | 5.5–9% | 4–5.9% |  |  |  |

### Structure

[edit | edit source]

Although milk looks simple to the eye, at the microscopic level, it has quite a complex structure.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) The aqueous (watery) phase forms the bulk of the milk, in which many other components are dissolved or dispersed. Water-soluble components, such as sugars, salts, and some proteins, are dissolved in this aqueous phase.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[17]](#cite_note-:13-17) Fat, on the other hand, does not typically combine well with water. To remain dispersed within the aqueous phase, the fat takes the form of tiny globules surrounded by a coating of certain proteins and phospholipids.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[17]](#cite_note-:13-17)[[20]](#cite_note-:12-20) This coating makes the fat globules repel each other, which prevents them from all merging together and creates an emulsion of the fat in the water.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) The globule coating also protects the facts from the action of certain enzymes in milk that would digest them.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Caseins also cluster together in tiny structures called micelles associated with calcium and phosphorus.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[21]](#cite_note-:22-21)[[22]](#cite_note-:11-22) These micelles have a charged exterior that makes the micelles repel each other and keeps them well-distributed in the aqueous phase as well.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[22]](#cite_note-:11-22)

### Appearance

[edit | edit source]

Because the casein micelles and fat globules in milk scatter light, milk is generally opaque and white in appearance.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)[[20]](#cite_note-:12-20) Milk with a higher proportion of fat has a whiter and more opaque appearance.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) The fat can also contain carotene, which adds a yellowish hue to the milk,[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11) and more fat results in more yellow color here.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) Some animals like goat, sheep, and buffalo have low to no carotene content and therefore produce whiter milk.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8) Riboflavin contributes a greenish color, which is especially apparent in whey.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[11]](#cite_note-:10-11)

### Flavor

[edit | edit source]

On the whole, milk is quite mild in taste and aroma,[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) with a subtle sweetness from its lactose content.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Much of the flavor is determined by the animal's diet, with pasture-raised animals generally producing more complexly flavored milk.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Many flavorful compounds are contained within the fatty component of milk,[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) making fattier milk more flavorful.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) If fresh, raw milk containing enzymes is agitated excessively, the fat globules are damaged and exposed to enzymes that digest the fat, yielding off-flavors.[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) When heated beyond 74–78°C (163–172°F), sulfur and nitrogen components in the protein can react with the environment to produce small quantities of hydrogen sulfide and ammonia,[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[20]](#cite_note-:12-20) both of which contribute to the cooked milk aroma.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)

### Heating

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:%22Boiled_Milk%22.jpg)

Milk bubbling up after extended boiling

Milk is generally quite tolerant of heating, the fat globules being sturdy enough not to pool and melt together, allowing milk to be significantly reduced by boiling.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Heating milk does result in a thin skin at the surface, which is the product of water evaporation concentrating protein at the surface.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) A similar effect occurs at the bottom of the pan by the heat source, resulting in sticking and scorching.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) When boiling milk, it's important to keep an eye on it and lower the heat as necessary to prevent it from boiling over the top of the pot.

### Curdling

[edit | edit source]

Under various conditions, proteins in milk can become denatured and tangled together, resulting in curdling.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) Increasingly acidic conditions disrupt the stability of casein micelles, causing them to denature and tangle together,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) separating from the aqueous phase.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) Mild acidification and relatively low temperature, as in simple lactic acid fermentation,[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) causes only a general thickening of the milk without much separation,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) with mild flocculation possible under some circumstances.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) The application of high or prolonged heating or tannins can intensify curdling.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10) For example, only slightly acidified milk can curdle when added to hot tea.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Certain enzymes, such as rennin, will also cause curdling of milk,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) though by a slightly different mechanism. Curdling—and its extent—may be desirable or undesirable, depending on the dish. It is critical to cheese-making[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) but usually avoided in the preparation of smooth custards, for example.[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19)

Higher milk fat content is more protective against curdling—this is why milk curdles more readily and significantly than does cream.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) The addition of starch can also act as a stabilizer,[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10) and tempering milk to prevent temperature shock is also reported to help.[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10) Once it occurs, however, curdling is irreversible.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)

## Production

[edit | edit source]

In large dairy production facilities, milk from a very large number of animals is pooled and processed together before undergoing any of the following processes.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)

### Pasteurization

[edit | edit source]

When raw (i.e. straight from the animal),[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10) milk has high enzymatic activity and is very susceptible to microbial contamination,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) leading to souring or spoilage. In order to preserve the milk in its fresh, fluid state and reduce the risk of food-borne illness and off-flavors, commercially available milk is often pasteurized by heating, which kills microbes and deactivates enzymes.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Note, however, that pasteurization will also destroy the "good" (i.e. lactic acid) bacteria that are required to produce cultured dairy products,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) so spoiled pasteurized milk instead putrefies. The table below details several types of pasteurization used for fluid milk.

| Type | Temperature | Time | Storage requirement | Shelf life | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Low Temperature, Long Time (LTLT) | 143–150°F / 62–65°C[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) | 30–35 minutes[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) | Refrigeration[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) |  | Very mild effect on flavor but may not kill heat-resistant microbes;[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) can be done in discrete batches, |
| High Temperature, Short Time (HTST) | 161–171°F / 72–77°C[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[24]](#cite_note-:14-24) | 15 seconds[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[24]](#cite_note-:14-24) | Refrigeration[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) | 2–3 weeks[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) | Mildly cooked flavor;[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) requires specialized industrial equipment to rapidly heat and chill the milk.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) |
| Ultra-high Temperature (UHT) | 265–300°F (130–150°C)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19)[[24]](#cite_note-:14-24) | 1–6 seconds[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19)[[24]](#cite_note-:14-24) | Refrigeration if not sterile-packaged;[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) room-temperature if in sterile packaging.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[24]](#cite_note-:14-24) | 50–60 days in fridge;[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) 6–10 months in sterile packaging[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) | Confers a cooked flavor;[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) some loss of vitamins;[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) requires specialized equipment as with HTST. |
| Sterilized (discontinuously) | 230–250°F (110–121°C)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) | 8–30 minutes[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) | Room temperature in sterile packaging | Indefinite (months to years)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[23]](#cite_note-:6-23) | Dark and strong flavor[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) |

Different jurisdictions globally may have different regulations about milk pasteurization. For example, some regions may not allow the use of raw milk unless for very aged cheeses.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19)

### Fat separation (creaming)

[edit") | edit source")]

If unhomogenized milk is left to stand, fat globules that are sufficiently large will rise to the surface due to their lower density than the rest of the milk.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[25]](#cite_note-:5-25) This results in a layer of high-fat cream on the surface of the milk,[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) and the cream may be skimmed off for separate use or to reduce the fat content of the milk.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) The cream may also be added back to the milk in controlled amounts to achieve a desired final fat content.[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) When left to simple gravity, the creaming process will take about 12–24 hours;[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) industrial dairying uses centrifugation to speed this process.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)

Interestingly, the fat globules in goat, sheep, and buffalo milk are naturally so small that they will not readily separate out.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)

### Homogenization

[edit | edit source]

If it is important to prevent creaming, milk may be homogenized.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15) In this process, the milk is forced through tiny holes at high pressure, which shears the large globules into smaller globules.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16) Because the smaller globules are not fully covered by their membrane,[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) casein micelles coat the surface, which holds the globules in emulsion and keeps them from merging together or floating back to the surface.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[24]](#cite_note-:14-24) After homogenization, the milk globules are 0.2–2 µm across, down from 1—10 µm.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)

Homogenization is common for commercially sold milk by industrial dairies. It results in a whiter color and richer—though slightly less flavorful—taste.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[17]](#cite_note-:13-17)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) Homogenized milk has a greater resistance to off-flavors,[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) but it froths and curdles more easily.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)

Pasteurization is done before or during homogenization[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)—if raw milk is homogenized, the intact enzymes in the milk will break down the exposed fat globules and produce off-flavors.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)

### Concentration

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:Milk_powder_cropped.jpg)

Powdered milk

The production of various concentrated milk products is achieved by removing a large portion of the water.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Evaporated and sweetened condensed milks significantly reduce the water content but leave the milk as a fluid.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) Dry milk powder is made by removing all the water through either spray- or freeze-drying,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) resulting in a concentrated, shelf-stable product.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The flavor is not the same as that of fresh milk.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) It can be made from skim or whole milk,[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) but the fat in whole milk powder makes it susceptible to rancidity and thus requiring of refrigeration.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Milk powder can also be purchased at different degrees of heat treatment—this is important in some baking recipes, as high heat treatment is necessary to denature components (e.g. glutathione, globulins) that reduce gluten development and gas bubble stability.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Fortification

[edit | edit source]

Because some of the above processing steps reduce milk's vitamin content, milk may be re-fortified with various vitamins (e.g. A and D).[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19)[[27]](#cite_note-:9-27) Low-fat milks may have additional milk solids added to make the texture less thin.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Milk may also be flavored and/or sweetened, as in chocolate milk.[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

If an English-language recipe calls for "milk" with no other qualifiers, it is typically referring to whole cow's milk.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13) Using a milk other than the one called for by a recipe can result in changes to flavor and texture due to the differing composition.

Fluid milk is an extremely perishable product and must be refrigerated below 40°F (4°C) unless specifically treated to be shelf-stable.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:20-10)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) Freezing is not recommended, since the crystals that form break the emulsion and result in the fat pooling/clumping upon thawing.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) Milk should be stored away from light, which causes chemical breakdown and unpleasant flavors over time.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) Nonfat milk powder can be stored in an airtight container at room temperature away from light, heat, and moisture.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) It may darken or clump over time.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

A wide range of dairy products, both fresh and cultured, are derived from milk,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) but it has extensive cooking applications on its own.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Milk powder is used to improve browning, flavor, texture, and shelf-life in a number of confections and baked goods,[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12)[[15]](#cite_note-:18-15)[[19]](#cite_note-:19-19) as it contributes protein, sugar, and salts.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) Fluid milk offers similar benefits,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[16]](#cite_note-:8-16) though with a higher water component, which adds moisture.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) As mentioned above, milk used in baked goods is best heated to at least 180 °F (82 °C) in order to denature components that negatively affect texture.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12) If fresh milk flavor is critical (e.g. in some custards and sauces), fluid milk should be used over dry.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)

### Dietary tolerance

[edit | edit source]

The sugar present in milk is lactose, which requires a specialized enzyme (lactase) in the human body to digest it.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) If someone without the lactase enzyme drinks milk, the lactose will not be properly digested by the body; instead, human gut microbes will break it down, which causes unpleasant gastrointestinal side effects.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Human babies naturally make their own lactase, but humans usually stop producing lactase over time, with most adults becoming "lactose intolerant".[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Some populations and individuals retain lactase activity into adulthood, but this is not the norm.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) This means that many people cannot consume fresh milk without negative side effects. Dairy products that have low to no lactose content, such as cultured dairy products where the lactose has already been digested by the fermentation microbes, are generally safe to consume.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) Additionally, lactase enzyme can be added directly to milk to break down the lactose prior to consumption,[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[14]](#cite_note-:15-14) and an oral supplement of lactase can be taken before consuming milk products for a similar effect.

Other sources of dietary intolerance to milk include milk proteins—this cannot be alleviated to the same extent as lactose intolerance.

## Techniques

[edit | edit source]

### Foaming

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:Steamed_milk.jpg)

Foaming milk with an espresso machine

The proteins in fluid milk give it the ability to create foams,[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9) much as with egg white. However, the foam created by milk is less stable than egg white foam due to its lower overall protein content.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) To create an especially stable milk foam, hot steam is injected to heat and agitate the milk with air.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) Heating the milk denatures some of its proteins, which then stabilize the tiny air bubbles,[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) but overheating the milk for too long while foaming will destabilize the liquid phase and result in a weak foam.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) As with egg white foams, fat interferes with the protein's ability to stabilize air bubbles,[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3) so low-fat milks foam better.[[3]](#cite_note-:16-3)[[22]](#cite_note-:11-22) Milks fortified with protein will also foam better,[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) making evaporated milk foam particularly well.[[9]](#cite_note-:21-9)

### Souring

[edit | edit source]

Lactic acid-producing bacteria have a particular affinity for milk and can readily feed on its lactose, which they convert to lactic acid.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5) This domination and acid production makes the milk less hospitable to bacteria that cause spoilage, putrefaction, and food-borne illness[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)—hence, cultured milk products (e.g. yogurt, cheese, kefir, etc) have historically been used to extend the shelf life of milk products.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Lactic acid bacteria are often present naturally in milk, but adding a starter culture boosts their presence, especially in pasteurized milk products.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Plant milks can sometimes be substituted for animal milk in recipes;[[25]](#cite_note-:5-25)[[28]](#cite_note-28) similarly, water, and other types of dairy milk can be reasonably substituted for each other (depending on the dish),[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12) although these all can impact the flavour or texture of the resulting dish.[[5]](#cite_note-:17-5)[[12]](#cite_note-:23-12)
